document.title: Product Requirements Document: Alloy Framework TypeSpec Emitter

product_overview.name: @microsoft/http-client-js
product_overview.description: A TypeSpec-to-TypeScript code emitter built on the Alloy Framework that generates modular TypeScript client SDKs for REST services. This is a ground-up rewrite of the legacy autorest.typescript/typespec-ts emitter, replacing ts-morph imperative AST manipulation with declarative Alloy JSX components.
product_overview.problem_statement: The legacy emitter (autorest.typescript/packages/typespec-ts) suffers from: (1) overly complex code using raw string concatenation and manual import management, (2) fragile string-based reference resolution causing self-import bugs, (3) monolithic helper files (2700+ lines) that are hard to maintain, (4) no framework for symbol tracking or cross-file reference management. These issues increase maintenance cost, slow feature development, and introduce subtle bugs.
product_overview.solution: Rewrite the emitter using Alloy Framework JSX components with automatic symbol resolution, refkey-based cross-file references, and composable component architecture. The rewrite must produce output-identical TypeScript SDKs to prevent breaking changes for existing Azure SDK consumers.
target_users: TypeSpec SDK authors generating TypeScript client libraries
Azure SDK team generating Azure service client SDKs
Internal Microsoft teams generating REST client code from TypeSpec definitions

goals.title: Output Parity
goals.description: Generated TypeScript SDK code must be functionally identical to the legacy emitter output. The public API surface (exported types, function signatures, class shapes) must not change.
goals.success_metric: All 101 scenario tests pass with matching output (modulo whitespace and import ordering)

goals.title: Pure Alloy Architecture
goals.description: The entire emitter must use idiomatic Alloy patterns: JSX components, refkeys for all declarations/references, code tagged templates, context providers. No legacy bridges, no string concatenation for imports, no manual path calculation.
goals.success_metric: Zero instances of manual import string construction or string-based reference resolution in codebase

goals.title: Composable Azure Extension
goals.description: Core emitter works standalone for non-Azure scenarios. Azure-specific features (auth, logging, polling, cloud settings) are added via JSX composition, not conditional flags.
goals.success_metric: Core emitter generates valid non-Azure SDK; Azure wrapper adds Azure-specific features without modifying core components

goals.title: Maintainability
goals.description: Dramatically reduce code complexity compared to legacy emitter. Each component has single responsibility, is independently testable, and is under 300 lines.
goals.success_metric: No component file exceeds 300 lines; each component has at least one test

goals.title: Developer Experience
goals.description: New components are easy to add and test. The component architecture makes it straightforward to extend the emitter for new features.
goals.success_metric: Adding a new feature (e.g., new serialization format) requires creating a new component, not modifying existing ones

non_goals.title: RLC (Rest Level Client) Rewrite
non_goals.description: The RLC code path will NOT be ported. Only the modular path is being rewritten.

non_goals.title: typespec-test Package
non_goals.description: The integration test package (typespec-test) will not be ported in this phase.

non_goals.title: Behavioral Changes
non_goals.description: This rewrite must NOT introduce behavioral changes to generated code. Output parity is the primary constraint.

non_goals.title: New Features
non_goals.description: No new code generation features will be added during the rewrite. Feature parity only.

non_goals.title: Runtime Library Changes
non_goals.description: No changes to @typespec/ts-http-runtime or Azure runtime packages.

non_functional.title: Output Parity
non_functional.description: Generated TypeScript code must match legacy emitter output. All 101 scenario tests must pass.

non_functional.title: Performance
non_functional.description: Emitter performance should be comparable to or better than legacy emitter for typical service specs.

non_functional.title: Code Quality
non_functional.description: All components follow Alloy idiomatic patterns. No anti-patterns (string-based refs, manual imports, monolithic code blocks).

non_functional.title: Testability
non_functional.description: Every component is independently testable with the Alloy testing utilities (toRenderTo matcher, TypeSpec test host).

non_functional.title: Extensibility
non_functional.description: Azure-specific features addable via JSX composition without modifying core components.

framework.name: Alloy Framework
framework.description: JSX-based code generation framework. NOT React. Uses JSX purely as structural description language for code output. Provides automatic symbol resolution, cross-file import management, and naming policies.

sdk_model.name: TCGC (TypeSpec Client Generator Core)
sdk_model.description: Provides SdkPackage object model that pre-computes data from TypeSpec definitions. Language-agnostic SDK type system with clients, operations, models, enums, and type metadata.

technical_architecture.data_flow: TypeSpec Input → Compiler → TCGC SdkPackage → Alloy JSX Components → Symbol Tree → TypeScript Files

key_concepts.refkeys: Symbolic reference keys created via refkey(entity, discriminator). Each declaration gets a refkey; references use the same refkey. Alloy resolves cross-file references and auto-generates imports.
key_concepts.code_templates: Tagged template literals (code`...`) that treat interpolated refkeys as live symbolic references. Alloy tracks them and resolves imports automatically.
key_concepts.context_providers: Named contexts via createNamedContext() for dependency injection. Eliminates prop drilling across deeply nested component trees.
key_concepts.external_packages: NPM packages defined via createPackage() with typed symbol accessors. Used in code templates for auto-import from runtime libraries.

test_categories.description: Markdown-based tests ported from legacy emitter. Each .md file contains TypeSpec input and expected TypeScript output blocks. Tests validate output parity between legacy and new emitter.
test_categories.format: H1 = test name, ```tsp = TypeSpec input, ```typescript <type> = expected output

test_categories.description: JSX component tests using Alloy's toRenderTo matcher. Compile TypeSpec snippets, render components, assert output text.
Pure function tests for utilities (refkey helpers, type mapping, etc.).

End-to-end tests ported from legacy emitter's modularIntegration suite. Each test generates a client library from a Spector TypeSpec spec, then exercises the generated client against the Spector mock server to validate runtime behavior. Uses Vitest with globalSetup for server lifecycle.

test_infrastructure.test_host: test/test-host.ts — Tester instance with TypeSpec library imports
test_infrastructure.scenario_harness: test/scenarios/scenarios.spec.ts — MD file parser + assertion engine

unacceptable_differences: Public API surface changes (exported types, function signatures)

| `headersRepeatability.spec.ts` | `special-headers/repeatability` | `test/e2e/http/special-headers/repeatability/main.test.ts` |

## Notes on versioning tests
The `versioning/removed` spec generates 3 different client variants based on version configuration:
- v1: Only the operations/types from version 1
- v2: Operations/types up to version 2 (v1 removed items are gone)
- v2preview: Preview version with additional preview operations

In the legacy emitter, these are 3 separate entries in cadl-ranch-list.js with different outputPaths but the same inputPath. In the new emitter, the generation script needs to compile the same spec 3 times with different version options. This may require special handling in the emit-e2e script or version-specific tspconfig.yaml files.

## Reference files
- Legacy tests: `submodules/autorest.typescript/packages/typespec-ts/test/modularIntegration/versioning*.spec.ts`, `specialWords.spec.ts`, `headersRepeatability.spec.ts`
- Legacy cadl-ranch-list.js: Shows how versioning/removed is split into 3 entries with different outputPaths

tasks.title: Add e2e test CI integration and coverage reporting
tasks.description:
Integrate the Spector e2e tests into the CI pipeline and set up coverage reporting.

## What to do
1. Add a CI step that runs `pnpm build && pnpm test:e2e` in the existing CI workflow
2. Ensure the Spector coverage file (`temp/spector-coverage.json`) is captured as a CI artifact
3. Add a script to parse and summarize coverage: which Spector scenarios are covered vs not

## Coverage reporting
After tests complete, the Spector server writes coverage data to `temp/spector-coverage.json`. This file tracks which mock API scenarios were hit during testing.

Create `eng/scripts/calculate-coverage.ts` that:
1. Reads `temp/spector-coverage.json`
2. Prints a summary: N/M scenarios covered
3. Lists uncovered scenarios

## Reference
- `submodules/typespec/packages/http-client-js/eng/scripts/calculate-coverage.js` — reference implementation
- Spector coverage format: `CoverageReport[]` from `@typespec/spector`

## Package.json script
Add: `"test:e2e:ci": "pnpm build && pnpm test:e2e"`

## Validation
- CI runs e2e tests successfully
- Coverage artifact is captured

tasks.title: Fix Content-Type for bare scalar body parameters (text/plain vs application/json)
tasks.description:
Fix 6 skipped e2e tests caused by incorrect Content-Type header for PUT operations on bare scalar types.

## Problem

When a TypeSpec operation accepts a bare scalar body (string, enum, unknown — not wrapped in a model), the Spector mock server expects `Content-Type: text/plain`. The emitter always generates `contentType: "application/json"` regardless of body type.

## Affected Tests (6)

| Test file | Test name | Line |
|-----------|-----------|------|
| `test/e2e/http/type/enum/fixed/main.test.ts` | should put a known enum value | 32 |
| `test/e2e/http/type/enum/fixed/main.test.ts` | should put an unknown enum value and receive 500 | 37 |
| `test/e2e/http/type/enum/extensible/main.test.ts` | should put a known enum value | 34 |
| `test/e2e/http/type/enum/extensible/main.test.ts` | should put an unknown enum value | 39 |
| `test/e2e/http/type/scalar/main.test.ts` | should put a string value | 29 |
| `test/e2e/http/type/scalar/main.test.ts` | should put an unknown value | 52 |

## Mock Server Expectations (from mockapi.ts)

- `type/enum/fixed` mockapi.ts: PUT expects `"Content-Type": "application/json"` with `json("Monday")`
- `type/enum/extensible` mockapi.ts: PUT expects `"Content-Type": "text/plain"` with `json("Monday")`
- `type/scalar/string` mockapi.ts: PUT expects `"Content-Type": "text/plain"` with `json("test")`
- `type/scalar/unknown` mockapi.ts: PUT expects `"Content-Type": "text/plain"` with `json("test")`

## Current Generated Code (broken)

All PUT operations generate: `contentType: "application/json", body: body`

For example, in `test/e2e/generated/type/enum/extensible/src/api/operations.ts`:
```typescript
return context.path("/type/enum/extensible/string/known-value").put({
  contentType: "application/json",
  body: body,
});
```

## Root Cause

The emitter operation generation component always emits `contentType: "application/json"` without checking whether the body is a bare scalar type. When the body is a bare scalar (not a model/object), the content-type should be `text/plain` per the TypeSpec HTTP specification.

## Recommended Fix

In the operation _send function generation code (likely in `src/components/` — look for where `contentType` is set in the request options), detect when the request body parameter type is a bare scalar:

1. Check the TCGC `SdkHttpOperation`'s body parameter type via `operation.bodyParam.type.kind`
2. If the body type kind is `"string"`, `"enum"`, `"unknown"`, or another scalar type (not `"model"`, `"array"`, `"dict"`), emit `contentType: "text/plain"`
3. Otherwise, keep `contentType: "application/json"`

The TCGC body parameter has a `type` field with a `kind` property that distinguishes scalars from complex types.

## After Fix

Remove `it.skip` from all 6 tests and verify they pass against the Spector mock server.

## Validation

```bash
pnpm build && pnpm test:e2e -- --grep "Type.Enum.Fixed|Type.Enum.Extensible|Type.Scalar"
```

tasks.title: Fix query parameter type coercion for Date, duration, and bytes types
tasks.description:
Fix 13 skipped e2e tests caused by raw typed objects (Date, number, Uint8Array) being passed directly to `expandUrlTemplate()` without encoding conversion.

## Problem

The emitter passes raw JavaScript objects to URL template expansion without converting them to the wire-format string. `Date` objects serialize to `[object Object]`, `Uint8Array` objects to `[object Uint8Array]`, etc.

## Affected Tests (13)

### Datetime Query Params (5)
| Test file | Test name | Line | Expected encoding |
|-----------|-----------|------|-------------------|
| `encode/datetime/main.test.ts` | default (rfc3339) query param | 21 | `value.toISOString()` → `2022-08-26T18:38:00.000Z` |
| `encode/datetime/main.test.ts` | rfc3339 query param | 25 | `value.toISOString()` → `2022-08-26T18:38:00.000Z` |
| `encode/datetime/main.test.ts` | rfc7231 query param | 29 | `value.toUTCString()` → `Fri, 26 Aug 2022 14:38:00 GMT` |
| `encode/datetime/main.test.ts` | unixTimestamp query param | 33 | `Math.floor(value.getTime()/1000)` → `1686566864` |
| `encode/datetime/main.test.ts` | unixTimestamp array query param | 37 | Each element unix-converted then CSV-joined |

### Duration Query Params (4)
| Test file | Test name | Line | Expected encoding |
|-----------|-----------|------|-------------------|
| `encode/duration/main.test.ts` | int32 seconds query param | 24 | `input=36` (numeric to string) |
| `encode/duration/main.test.ts` | float seconds query param | 28 | `input=35.625` |
| `encode/duration/main.test.ts` | float64 seconds query param | 32 | `input=35.625` |
| `encode/duration/main.test.ts` | int32 seconds array query param | 36 | `input=36,47` (CSV joined) |

### Bytes Query Params (4)
| Test file | Test name | Line | Expected encoding |
|-----------|-----------|------|-------------------|
| `encode/bytes/main.test.ts` | default (base64) query param | 51 | `uint8ArrayToString(value, "base64")` → `dGVzdA==` |
| `encode/bytes/main.test.ts` | base64 query param | 55 | `uint8ArrayToString(value, "base64")` → `dGVzdA==` |
| `encode/bytes/main.test.ts` | base64url query param | 59 | `uint8ArrayToString(value, "base64url")` → `dGVzdA` |
| `encode/bytes/main.test.ts` | base64url array query param | 63 | Each element base64url-encoded then CSV-joined |

## Current Generated Code (broken)

In `test/e2e/generated/encode/datetime/src/api/query/operations.ts`:
```typescript
const path = expandUrlTemplate("/encode/datetime/query/default{?value}", { "value": value });
// value is a Date object — serializes to garbage in URL
```

In `test/e2e/generated/encode/bytes/src/api/query/operations.ts`:
```typescript
const path = expandUrlTemplate("/encode/bytes/query/default{?value}", { "value": value });
// value is a Uint8Array — serializes to "[object Uint8Array]"
```

## Mock Server Expectations

From `node_modules/@typespec/http-specs/specs/encode/datetime/mockapi.ts`:
- `Encode_Datetime_Query_default`: expects query `value=2022-08-26T18:38:00.000Z` (rfc3339)
- `Encode_Datetime_Query_rfc7231`: expects `value=Fri, 26 Aug 2022 14:38:00 GMT`
- `Encode_Datetime_Query_unixTimestamp`: expects `value=1686566864` (unix seconds, numeric)

From `node_modules/@typespec/http-specs/specs/encode/bytes/mockapi.ts`:
- `Encode_Bytes_Query_default`: expects `value=dGVzdA==`
- `Encode_Bytes_Query_base64url`: expects `value=dGVzdA`

From `node_modules/@typespec/http-specs/specs/encode/duration/mockapi.ts`:
- `Encode_Duration_Query_int32Seconds`: expects `input=36`
- `Encode_Duration_Query_floatSeconds`: expects `input=35.625`

## Root Cause

The emitter's operation `_send` function generation passes raw parameter values into `expandUrlTemplate()` without applying the encoding transformation. TCGC provides `encode` info on each parameter's type (e.g., `type.encode === "rfc3339"`, `type.encode === "unixTimestamp"`, `type.encode === "base64url"`), but the operation generation code doesn't use it for query parameters.

## Recommended Fix

In the emitter's operation generation code that builds the URL template variables object:

1. For each query parameter, check `param.type.kind` and `param.type.encode`:
   - `utcDateTime` + `rfc3339` (or default): Generate `value.toISOString()`
   - `utcDateTime` + `rfc7231`: Generate `value.toUTCString()`
   - `utcDateTime` + `unixTimestamp`: Generate `Math.floor(value.getTime() / 1000)`
   - `duration` + numeric encoding: Generate `value` (number auto-stringified by URL)
   - `bytes` + `base64`: Generate `uint8ArrayToString(value, "base64")` (import from runtime)
   - `bytes` + `base64url`: Generate `uint8ArrayToString(value, "base64url")`
2. For array params, apply element-level encoding before joining with `buildCsvCollection`

Look at how the emitter handles these encodings for JSON body properties in `src/components/serialization/json-serializer.tsx` — apply the same pattern.

## Key Files to Investigate

- `src/components/` — find where `expandUrlTemplate` call is generated with query params
- `src/components/serialization/json-serializer.tsx` — existing encoding logic to reuse
- `test/e2e/generated/encode/datetime/src/api/query/operations.ts` — see current broken output

## After Fix

Remove `it.skip` from all 13 tests. Regenerate test clients if needed.

## Validation

```bash
pnpm build && pnpm test:e2e -- --grep "Encode.Datetime|Encode.Duration|Encode.Bytes"
```

tasks.title: Fix header parameter type coercion for Date, duration, and bytes types
tasks.description:
Fix 9 skipped e2e tests caused by raw typed objects (Date arrays, numbers, Uint8Array) being passed directly into HTTP headers without string conversion.

## Problem

The emitter generates header assignments that pass raw typed objects instead of string-encoded values. HTTP headers require string values.

## Affected Tests (9)

### Datetime Headers (2)
| Test file | Test name | Line | Expected |
|-----------|-----------|------|----------|
| `encode/datetime/main.test.ts` | unixTimestamp header | 94 | `Math.floor(value.getTime()/1000).toString()` → `"1686566864"` |
| `encode/datetime/main.test.ts` | unixTimestamp array header | 98 | Each element unix-converted, then CSV-joined → `"1686566864,1686734256"` |

### Duration Headers (3)
| Test file | Test name | Line | Expected |
|-----------|-----------|------|----------|
| `encode/duration/main.test.ts` | int32 seconds header | 104 | `duration.toString()` → `"36"` |
| `encode/duration/main.test.ts` | float seconds header | 108 | `duration.toString()` → `"35.625"` |
| `encode/duration/main.test.ts` | float64 seconds header | 112 | `duration.toString()` → `"35.625"` |

### Bytes Headers (4)
| Test file | Test name | Line | Expected |
|-----------|-----------|------|----------|
| `encode/bytes/main.test.ts` | default (base64) header | 103 | `uint8ArrayToString(value, "base64")` → `"dGVzdA=="` |
| `encode/bytes/main.test.ts` | base64 header | 107 | `uint8ArrayToString(value, "base64")` → `"dGVzdA=="` |
| `encode/bytes/main.test.ts` | base64url header | 111 | `uint8ArrayToString(value, "base64url")` → `"dGVzdA"` |
| `encode/bytes/main.test.ts` | base64url array header | 115 | Each element base64url-encoded, CSV-joined → `"dGVzdA,dGVzdA"` |

## Current Generated Code (broken)

In `test/e2e/generated/encode/duration/src/api/header/operations.ts`:
```typescript
headers: { "duration": duration, ...options.requestOptions?.headers }
// duration is a number — HTTP headers require strings
```

In `test/e2e/generated/encode/bytes/src/api/header/operations.ts`:
```typescript
headers: { "value": value, ...options.requestOptions?.headers }
// value is a Uint8Array — headers need base64 string
```

In `test/e2e/generated/encode/datetime/src/api/header/operations.ts`:
```typescript
headers: { "value": buildCsvCollection(value), ...options.requestOptions?.headers }
// value is Date[] — buildCsvCollection needs string/number elements, not Date objects
```

## Mock Server Expectations

From duration mockapi.ts:
- `Encode_Duration_Header_int32Seconds`: expects `duration: "36"`
- `Encode_Duration_Header_floatSeconds`: expects `duration: "35.625"`

From bytes mockapi.ts:
- `Encode_Bytes_Header_default`: expects `value: "dGVzdA=="`
- `Encode_Bytes_Header_base64urlArray`: expects `value: "dGVzdA,dGVzdA"`

From datetime mockapi.ts:
- `Encode_Datetime_Header_unixTimestamp`: expects `value: "1686566864"`
- `Encode_Datetime_Header_unixTimestampArray`: expects `value: "1686566864,1686734256"`

## Root Cause

Same underlying issue as E2E-FIX-QUERY-PARAM-ENCODING — the emitter doesn't apply `@encode` transformations when generating header parameter assignments. Headers require string values but the emitter passes raw SDK types (number, Date, Uint8Array) directly.

## Recommended Fix

In the emitter's operation generation code that builds the headers object, apply encoding based on param type:
- `utcDateTime` + `unixTimestamp`: `String(Math.floor(value.getTime() / 1000))`
- `duration` + numeric encoding: `value.toString()`
- `bytes` + `base64`/`base64url`: `uint8ArrayToString(value, encoding)`
- For array headers, encode each element before passing to `buildCsvCollection`

Coordinate with E2E-FIX-QUERY-PARAM-ENCODING since encoding logic is identical — consider extracting a shared helper.

## Key Files

- Same emitter operation generation code as query params
- `test/e2e/generated/encode/*/src/api/header/operations.ts` — current broken output

## Validation

```bash
pnpm build && pnpm test:e2e -- --grep "Encode.Datetime|Encode.Duration|Encode.Bytes"
```

tasks.title: Generate response header deserialization for operations with typed response headers
tasks.description:
Fix 4 skipped e2e tests caused by missing response header extraction and deserialization.

## Problem

Operations that return typed response headers generate `void` return types and no code to extract/parse header values.

## Affected Tests (4)

| Test file | Test name | Line | Expected header |
|-----------|-----------|------|----------------|
| `encode/datetime/main.test.ts` | default (rfc7231) response header | 119 | `value: "Fri, 26 Aug 2022 14:38:00 GMT"` → `Date` |
| `encode/datetime/main.test.ts` | rfc3339 response header | 131 | `value: "2022-08-26T18:38:00.000Z"` → `Date` |
| `encode/datetime/main.test.ts` | rfc7231 response header | 143 | `value: "Fri, 26 Aug 2022 14:38:00 GMT"` → `Date` |
| `encode/datetime/main.test.ts` | unixTimestamp response header | 155 | `value: "1686566864"` → `Date` (via `new Date(parseInt(v)*1000)`) |

## Current Generated Code (broken)

In `test/e2e/generated/encode/datetime/src/api/responseHeader/operations.ts`:
```typescript
export async function $default(
  context: DatetimeContext,
  options: DefaultOptionalParams = { requestOptions: {} },
): Promise<void> {  // Returns void — should return { value: Date }
  const result = await _defaultSend(context, options);
  return _defaultDeserialize(result);
}

export async function _defaultDeserialize(
  result: PathUncheckedResponse,
): Promise<void> {
  const expectedStatuses = ["204"];
  if (!expectedStatuses.includes(result.status)) {
    throw createRestError(result);
  }
  // No code to extract headers!
}
```

## Mock Server Expectations

From `node_modules/@typespec/http-specs/specs/encode/datetime/mockapi.ts`:
- `Encode_Datetime_ResponseHeader_default`: Returns response header `value: "Fri, 26 Aug 2022 14:38:00 GMT"` with status 204
- `Encode_Datetime_ResponseHeader_rfc3339`: Returns header `value: "2022-08-26T18:38:00.000Z"`
- `Encode_Datetime_ResponseHeader_unixTimestamp`: Returns header `value: "1686566864"` (unix seconds as string)

## Root Cause

The emitter's deserialize function generation doesn't handle response headers. When an operation's response includes `@header` properties, the emitter should generate a response type with header fields, extract values from `result.headers["name"]`, and apply type-appropriate deserialization.

## Recommended Fix

1. In operation deserialize function generation, check TCGC's `operation.responses[].headers` for response header definitions
2. If headers exist, generate a response model type with the header fields
3. In `_deserialize`, extract headers from `result.headers["headerName"]`
4. Apply encoding-aware deserialization:
   - `utcDateTime` + default/rfc7231: `new Date(result.headers["value"])`
   - `utcDateTime` + rfc3339: `new Date(result.headers["value"])`
   - `utcDateTime` + unixTimestamp: `new Date(parseInt(result.headers["value"]) * 1000)`
5. Change return type from `void` to response header model

## Key Files

- Look for where `_deserialize` functions are generated in the emitter
- Check how TCGC exposes response headers: `operation.responses[].headers`
- Reference: `submodules/flight-instructor/` for how response headers are handled

## Validation

```bash
pnpm build && pnpm test:e2e -- --grep "Encode.Datetime"
```

tasks.title: Handle @encode(string) on integer types in serialization/deserialization

| `encode/numeric/main.test.ts` | optional uint32 as string | 21 | `"1"` | `1` |
| `encode/numeric/main.test.ts` | uint8 as string | 27 | `"255"` | `255` |

## Current Generated Code (broken)

In `test/e2e/generated/encode/numeric/src/models/models.ts`:
```typescript
export interface SafeintAsStringProperty {
  value: string;  // Should be number
}
export function safeintAsStringPropertySerializer(item: SafeintAsStringProperty): any {
  return { value: item["value"] };  // No conversion
}
```

tasks.title: Fix unresolved refkey for extensible enum array serializers in getTypeSignature()
tasks.description:
Fix 12 skipped e2e tests caused by unresolved symbol references in generated extensible enum array serializers. This is a CRITICAL bug — the generated code contains literal `<Unresolved Symbol: ...>` text that causes esbuild parse failures, making the entire client unusable.

## Problem

The generated `models.ts` for encode/array contains broken code:
```typescript
export function commaDelimitedExtensibleEnumArrayPropertySerializer(...) {
  return {
    value: buildCsvCollection(<Unresolved Symbol: refkey[sarraySerializer⁣senum]>(item["value"])),
  };
}
```
This appears 4 times (lines 173, 181, 189, 197).

## Affected Tests (12)

All tests in `test/e2e/http/encode/array/main.test.ts` (entire `describe.skip` block):
- string array: comma, space, pipe, newline delimiter (4)
- enum array: comma, space, pipe, newline delimiter (4)
- extensible enum array: comma, space, pipe, newline delimiter (4)

## Mock Server Expectations

From `node_modules/@typespec/http-specs/specs/encode/array/mockapi.ts`:
Each test POSTs `{ value: "blue,red,green" }` (or space/pipe/newline delimited) and expects same echoed back.

## Root Cause

In `src/utils/refkeys.ts`, `getTypeSignature()` returns just `"enum"` for ALL enum types (regular and extensible). The execution flow:

1. Serializer generation encounters extensible enum array property
2. `getSerializationExpression()` checks `needsTransformation(type.valueType)` → true
3. `valueTypeHasNamedSerializerFn(type.valueType)` → true for extensible enums
4. Generates: `code\`\${arraySerializerRefkey(type.valueType)}(\${accessor})\``
5. `arraySerializerRefkey()` calls `refkey("arraySerializer", getTypeSignature(type.valueType))`
6. `getTypeSignature()` returns `"enum"` — NOT unique per enum type
7. No `JsonArraySerializer` component declared with matching refkey → UNRESOLVED

The specific code in `src/utils/refkeys.ts`:
```typescript
default:
  return t.kind;  // Returns "enum" for ALL enum types
```

## Recommended Fix

In `src/utils/refkeys.ts`, update `getTypeSignature()` for enum case:
```typescript
case "enum":
  return `enum:${t.name}`;  // e.g., "enum:ColorsExtensibleEnum"
```

This ensures each enum type gets a unique refkey. Then verify `JsonArraySerializer` in `src/components/serialization/json-array-record-helpers.tsx` is rendered for extensible enum types.

## Key Files

- `src/utils/refkeys.ts` — `getTypeSignature()` function
- `src/components/serialization/json-array-record-helpers.tsx` — `JsonArraySerializer` rendering
- `src/components/serialization/json-serializer.tsx` — `valueTypeHasNamedSerializerFn()`

## After Fix

Change `describe.skip` to `describe` in test. Regenerate test clients.

## Validation

```bash
pnpm build && pnpm test:e2e -- --grep "Encode.Array"
```

tasks.title: Generate additionalProperties bag in derived type serializers/deserializers
tasks.description:
Fix 16 skipped e2e tests caused by derived types losing their `additionalProperties` bag during serialization/deserialization.

## Problem

When a model extends a base type with additional properties (Record<string, unknown>), base type serializers/deserializers correctly use `serializeRecord()`/`deserializeRecord()`. But derived types do NOT include this logic, silently discarding additional properties.

## Affected Tests (16)

### Derived Unknown Additional Properties (4)
| Test file | Test name | Line |
|-----------|-----------|------|
| `type/property/additional-properties/main.test.ts` | get derived unknown additional props | 54 |
| `type/property/additional-properties/main.test.ts` | put derived unknown additional props | 59 |
| `type/property/additional-properties/main.test.ts` | get derived unknown additional props (is) | 113 |
| `type/property/additional-properties/main.test.ts` | put derived unknown additional props (is) | 118 |

### Discriminated Unknown Additional Properties (4)

|-----------|-----------|------|
| `type/property/additional-properties/main.test.ts` | get discriminated unknown additional props | 76 |
| `type/property/additional-properties/main.test.ts` | put discriminated unknown additional props | 81 |
| `type/property/additional-properties/main.test.ts` | get discriminated unknown additional props (is) | 133 |
| `type/property/additional-properties/main.test.ts` | put discriminated unknown additional props (is) | 138 |

### Different Spread Types (8)
| Test file | Test name | Line |
|-----------|-----------|------|
| `type/property/additional-properties/main.test.ts` | get/put derived different spread string | 419-424 |
| `type/property/additional-properties/main.test.ts` | get/put derived different spread float | 437-442 |
| `type/property/additional-properties/main.test.ts` | get/put derived different spread model | 455-460 |
| `type/property/additional-properties/main.test.ts` | get/put derived different spread model array | 473-478 |

## Current Generated Code

### Base type (WORKING):
```typescript
export function extendsUnknownAdditionalPropertiesDeserializer(item: any) {
  return {
    additionalProperties: deserializeRecord(item, undefined, ["name"]),
    name: item["name"],
  };
}
```

### Derived type (BROKEN):
```typescript
export function extendsUnknownAdditionalPropertiesDerivedDeserializer(item: any) {
  return {
    name: item["name"],
    index: item["index"],
    age: item["age"],
    // MISSING: additionalProperties: deserializeRecord(item, undefined, ["name", "index", "age"])
  };
}
```

## Mock Server Expectations

From `node_modules/@typespec/http-specs/specs/type/property/additional-properties/mockapi.ts`:
Tests expect round-trip of additional properties. E.g., derived type: `{ name: "abc", index: 1, age: 2.5, prop1: 32, prop2: true }` where prop1/prop2 are additional properties that must be preserved.

## Root Cause

In the serialization/deserialization components, when generating serializers for a model type, the code checks `type.additionalProperties` but doesn't walk the inheritance chain (`type.baseModel`) to detect inherited additional properties.

## Recommended Fix

In serializer/deserializer generation:
1. Walk the inheritance chain to detect inherited additional properties
2. Generate `serializeRecord()`/`deserializeRecord()` with combined known property names from full hierarchy

For serializers: `...serializeRecord(item["additionalProperties"] ?? {})`
For deserializers: `additionalProperties: deserializeRecord(item, valueDeserializer, ["name", "index", "age"])`

The 4 discriminated union tests are fixed automatically once derived deserializers are fixed.

## Key Files

- `src/components/serialization/json-serializer.tsx` — model serializer generation
- `src/components/serialization/json-deserializer.tsx` — model deserializer generation
- Check TCGC: `type.additionalProperties` vs `type.baseModel.additionalProperties`

## Validation

```bash
pnpm build && pnpm test:e2e -- --grep "AdditionalProperties"
```

tasks.title: Generate non-discriminated union deserialization for additional properties spread types

```typescript
export function _spreadRecordForNonDiscriminatedUnionAdditionalPropertyDeserializer(
  item: any,
): _SpreadRecordForNonDiscriminatedUnionAdditionalProperty {
  return item;  // No discrimination, no deserialization!
}
```

Union members include:
- `WidgetData0`: `{ kind: "kind0", fooProp: string }`
- `WidgetData1`: `{ kind: "kind1", start: Date, end?: Date }`
- `WidgetData2`: `{ kind: "kind1", start: string }` (overlapping discriminator!)

## Mock Server Expectations

The emitter doesn't generate type discrimination logic for non-discriminated unions. Members with overlapping `kind` fields need secondary property checks.

2. For overlapping `kind` values, use secondary property checks (e.g., presence of `end` field)
3. Apply member-specific deserialization (e.g., ISO date parsing)

This depends on E2E-FIX-ADDITIONAL-PROPS-DERIVED being completed first.

tasks.title: Fix parameter name shadowing operation function import in client class methods
tasks.description:
Fix 1 skipped e2e test caused by a generated method parameter shadowing an imported operation function.

## Problem

The generated `UsageClient` class has `input(input: InputRecord, ...)` where parameter `input` shadows imported function `input` from `./api/operations.js`. At runtime: `TypeError: input is not a function`.

## Affected Test (1)

In `test/e2e/generated/type/model/usage/src/usageClient.ts`:
```typescript
import { input, output, inputAndOutput } from "./api/operations.js";

export class UsageClient {
  input(
    input: InputRecord,  // Parameter "input" shadows imported function "input"
    options: InputOptionalParams = { requestOptions: {} },
  ): Promise<void> {
    return input(this._client, input, options);  // Calls parameter, NOT function!

}
```

In client class method generation, when method body calls `operationName(this._client, param, options)`, if any parameter has the same name as the operation function, JS scoping causes shadowing.

## Recommended Fix

Alternatively, import with aliases: `import { input as inputOp } from "./api/operations.js"`.

The existing name policy (`src/utils/name-policy.ts`) already handles reserved word conflicts — extend for operation name conflicts.

tasks.title: Add null guard in array serializer/deserializer for nullable model elements
tasks.description:

tasks.title: Map non-standard HTTP auth schemes to apiKey auth in client context generation
tasks.description:
Fix 2 skipped e2e tests caused by custom HTTP authentication schemes not being recognized by the runtime auth policy.

## Problem

The emitter generates `{ kind: "http", scheme: "sharedaccesskey" }` for custom HTTP auth, but the runtime only recognizes `kind: "apiKey"` for key-based auth and `kind: "http"` for basic/bearer.

## Affected Tests (2)

| Test file | Test name | Line |
|-----------|-----------|------|
| `authentication/http/custom/main.test.ts` | should authenticate with a valid custom key | 37 |
| `authentication/http/custom/main.test.ts` | should return error for an invalid custom key | 50 |

## Current Generated Code (broken)

In `test/e2e/generated/authentication/http/custom/src/customClientContext.ts`:
```typescript
return getClient(endpointUrl, {
  ...updatedOptions,
  credential,
  authSchemes: [{ kind: "http", scheme: "sharedaccesskey" }]  // Not recognized
});
```

Working comparison (API Key auth):
```typescript
authSchemes: [{ kind: "apiKey", apiKeyLocation: "header", name: "x-ms-api-key" }]  // Works
```

## Mock Server Expectations

From `node_modules/@typespec/http-specs/specs/authentication/http/custom/mockapi.ts`:
- Valid: GET `/authentication/http/custom/valid` with header `Authorization: SharedAccessKey valid-key` → 204
- Invalid: GET `/authentication/http/custom/invalid` with header `Authorization: SharedAccessKey invalid-key` → 403

## Root Cause

In `src/components/client-context.tsx`, `buildAuthSchemesLiteral()` has:
```typescript
case "http":
  return `{ kind: "http", scheme: "${scheme.scheme.toLowerCase()}" }`;
```
This generates `kind: "http"` for ALL HTTP schemes, but custom schemes need `kind: "apiKey"`.

## Recommended Fix

Update `buildAuthSchemesLiteral()` in `src/components/client-context.tsx`:
```typescript
case "http": {
  const schemeName = (scheme.scheme ?? "").toLowerCase();
  if (schemeName === "basic" || schemeName === "bearer") {
    return `{ kind: "http", scheme: "${schemeName}" }`;
  }
  return `{ kind: "apiKey", apiKeyLocation: "header", name: "Authorization" }`;
}
```

## Key Files

- `src/components/client-context.tsx` — `buildAuthSchemesLiteral()` function

## Validation

```bash
pnpm build && pnpm test:e2e -- --grep "Authentication.Http.Custom"
```

tasks.title: Fix createRestError crash on empty-body error responses (e.g., 500 with no body)
tasks.description:
Fix 1 skipped e2e test caused by runtime crash when handling error responses with no body.

## Problem

The `createRestError(result)` call in `@typespec/ts-http-runtime@0.2.1` crashes with `TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'message')` when `result.body` is undefined. This happens when a server returns an error status (e.g., 500) with no response body.

## Affected Tests (1)

| Test file | Test name | Line |
|-----------|-----------|------|
| `test/e2e/http/type/enum/fixed/main.test.ts` | should put an unknown enum value and receive 500 | 39 |

## Root Cause

In `createRestError(result)`, when `result.body` is undefined:
- `const internalError = resp.body?.error ?? resp.body` → undefined
- `internalError.message` → TypeError (accessing property of undefined)

The runtime function lacks a null check for `internalError` before accessing `.message`.

## Options

1. **Fix in runtime**: Add `internalError?.message` (preferred but requires upstream PR to ts-http-runtime)
2. **Fix in emitter**: Change generated error handling to use string overload: `createRestError('Unexpected status code: ' + result.status, result)` — but this changes all scenario test expectations
3. **Workaround**: Generate conditional: `throw result.body !== undefined ? createRestError(result) : createRestError('Unexpected status code: ' + result.status, result)`

## Validation

After fix, unskip the test and run:
```bash
pnpm build && pnpm test:e2e -- --grep "Type.Enum.Fixed"
```

tasks.title: Fix race condition in emit-e2e.ts for nested spec directories (type/union)
tasks.description:
Fix the flaky ENOTEMPTY race condition in eng/scripts/emit-e2e.ts when two specs share a parent-child output directory relationship.

## Problem
When `type/union` (output: `test/e2e/generated/type/union/`) and `type/union/discriminated` (output: `test/e2e/generated/type/union/discriminated/`) are compiled in parallel, a race condition occurs:
1. `type/union/discriminated` creates `type/union/discriminated/` via mkdir (also creating parent `type/union/`)
2. `type/union` tries to `rm(type/union, {recursive: true})` to clean the output directory
3. While rm is traversing the directory tree, `type/union/discriminated`'s tsp compile creates new files inside it
4. This causes `ENOTEMPTY: directory not empty, rmdir` error

This is non-deterministic — depends on parallel execution timing. Seen consistently in full CI runs but may pass with fewer specs or different timing.

## Fix approaches
1. **Serialize nested specs**: Detect parent-child directory relationships and ensure parent specs are compiled only after child specs complete
2. **Clean before parallel phase**: Do all directory cleanup in a serial pre-pass before starting any parallel compilation
3. **Retry with backoff**: Wrap the rm/mkdir in a retry loop (fragile, not recommended)

Approach 1 or 2 is recommended.

## Impact
Blocks reliable CI for e2e tests (SPECTOR-9 depends on this).

## Validation
Run `pnpm emit:e2e` 5+ times in a row — all should pass without ENOTEMPTY errors.

tasks.title: Fix ARM e2e tests: Bearer token auth rejected over non-TLS HTTP
tasks.description:
## Problem
Azure Resource Manager clients use `TokenCredential` (Bearer token auth) which requires HTTPS. The e2e mock server runs on `http://localhost:3002`, causing `@azure/core-rest-pipeline` to reject requests with:
```
Bearer token authentication is not permitted for non-TLS protected (non-https) URLs.
```

## Root Cause
The generated ARM clients enforce TLS for Bearer tokens (correct production behavior), but e2e tests need to bypass this for the HTTP mock server. Need either:
- A way to pass `allowInsecureConnection: true` that propagates to the bearer token policy
- Or a test-only credential that doesn't enforce TLS

## Affected Tests (8 tests across 3 files, all skipped)
- `test/e2e/http/azure/resource-manager/common-properties/main.test.ts` — 5 tests
- `test/e2e/http/azure/resource-manager/large-header/main.test.ts` — 1 test

Symptoms:
- Header params: `Expected test-name-value but got undefined`
- Query params: `Expected query param format=text but got undefined`
- Path params: `Unexpected status code: 404` (path segment missing from URL)

The emitter generates client constructors that accept these parameters but doesn't wire them into the request pipeline or URL template.

tasks.title: Fix spread parameter invalid TypeScript generation

design_review.problem: The emitter generates invalid TypeScript for spread parameters. When an operation uses @spread with header parameters, the generated interface signatures contain hyphenated names like `x-ms-test-header` which are not valid TypeScript identifiers. This blocks 11 Spector scenarios (all Parameters_Spread_Alias and Parameters_Spread_Model tests).

approaches.name: Normalize names in operation signature generation
approaches.description: Fix the operation/function declaration code in send-operation.tsx to apply camelCase normalization when generating TypeScript parameter names from TCGC SdkHttpParameter objects. The serializedName (wire name) would still be used for HTTP headers/query strings, but the TypeScript-facing name would be camelCased. This is a surgical fix in the parameter name mapping layer.
pros: Minimal change — only touches parameter name generation
Matches how other parameter types already work
Wire compatibility preserved automatically via serializedName
cons: Needs careful handling of name collisions after normalization
Must update both classic interface declarations and API layer functions

approaches.name: Apply name policy at the model-interface level
approaches.description: Change model-interface.tsx to run all property names through the emitter's name policy (createEmitterNamePolicy) when rendering InterfaceMember names. This catches not just spread parameters but any property with non-TS-safe names. The serializer/deserializer would map between the normalized name and the wire name.
pros: Catches ALL non-TS-safe property names globally
Consistent with existing name policy infrastructure
Future-proof for other property name edge cases
cons: Broader change surface — may affect working scenarios
Need to verify all serializer/deserializer property accessors still align
Risk of regressions in already-passing tests

design_review.chosen: Normalize names in operation signature generation
design_review.rationale: The surgical approach in send-operation.tsx is safer because it only affects spread parameters where the bug actually manifests. The model-interface approach risks regressions in the 648 already-passing tests. TCGC already provides a `name` field on method parameters that should be the TypeScript-safe name — the bug is that spread properties use the wire `serializedName` instead of the normalized `name`. The fix is to ensure spread property accessors use the parameter's TypeScript name, not its serialized name.

tasks.title: Fix string array delimiter encoding in JSON body properties

design_review.problem: String array properties with delimiter encoding (@encode with csv/ssv/tsv/pipes) call buildCsvCollection() in the JSON body serializer, producing a joined string like 'a,b,c'. The Spector mock API expects the delimited string in the body (this IS correct per the spec), but the e2e tests were written expecting a JSON array. The issue is a test expectation mismatch, not a serializer bug — OR the serializer should not apply delimiter encoding for body properties and the spec expects a raw array. Need to verify against the actual mock API behavior.

approaches.name: Fix the e2e test expectations to match spec behavior
approaches.description: The Spector spec for Encode_Array_Property defines body properties as delimiter-encoded strings. If the generated serializer correctly produces 'a,b,c' and the mock API accepts this, the fix is to update the test expectations to send/receive delimiter-encoded strings rather than arrays. Verify by running the tests without .skip and checking what the mock API returns.
pros: Aligns tests with actual spec behavior
cons: Must verify against actual mock API responses
If the mock API actually expects arrays, this approach is wrong

approaches.name: Remove delimiter encoding from body serializer path
approaches.description: Modify the JSON serializer to NOT apply buildCsvCollection() for body properties — delimiter encoding should only apply to query/header parameters. Body properties should pass arrays as-is (JSON arrays). The serializer would check the encoding context (body vs query vs header) before applying collection format.
pros: Semantically correct — JSON bodies should contain arrays, not delimiter-joined strings
Query and header paths continue to work correctly
cons: Requires modifying the serializer code path
Must verify the spec actually expects JSON arrays in body (not strings)
Need to check if enum arrays work differently

design_review.chosen: Investigate and verify against mock API, then apply the correct fix
design_review.rationale: Both approaches could be correct depending on what the Spector mock API actually expects. The first step is to remove the .skip, run the tests, and check the actual failure. The Spector spec uses @encode(ArrayEncoding.commaDelimited) on body properties, which semantically means the wire format should be a delimiter-joined string. If the mock API expects strings, fix the tests. If it expects arrays, fix the serializer. The enum array tests passing suggests the current serializer behavior may be correct for the spec.

tasks.title: Fix unix timestamp serialization for datetime body properties

design_review.problem: Datetime properties encoded as unixTimestamp in JSON body position produce numeric values via the JSON serializer (e.g., 1686566864), but the round-trip fails. Header and query positions work because they explicitly wrap with String(). The JSON serializer generates `((accessor).getTime() / 1000) | 0` which returns a number — this should be correct for JSON bodies (numbers are valid JSON). The actual failure mode needs investigation: is it a serialization issue (number format wrong) or a deserialization issue (response parsing fails)?

approaches.name: Fix the JSON deserializer to reconstruct Date from unix timestamp numbers
approaches.description: The serializer correctly converts Date → number. The likely issue is the deserializer: it may not convert the numeric unix timestamp back to a Date object. Add a case in json-deserializer.tsx for utcDateTime with unixTimestamp encoding that creates `new Date(value * 1000)` from the numeric response value.
cons: Need to verify this is actually the failure point
Must handle edge cases (null, undefined, arrays)

approaches.name: Generate both serializer and deserializer as string conversions
approaches.description: Change both serializer and deserializer to work with string representations of unix timestamps, matching the header/query behavior. Serializer would produce String(date.getTime() / 1000) and deserializer would parse Number() then construct Date.
pros: Simpler mental model — all unix timestamps are strings
cons: JSON bodies should contain numbers, not strings — this would be semantically wrong
Breaks JSON schema expectations
Unnecessary string conversion

design_review.chosen: Fix the JSON deserializer to reconstruct Date from unix timestamp numbers
design_review.rationale: JSON bodies should contain numeric values for unix timestamps (e.g., 1686566864, not '1686566864'). The serializer is likely correct. The deserializer needs to detect the unixTimestamp encoding and convert the response number back to a Date object via `new Date(value * 1000)`. This is the standard approach used by the legacy emitter and matches JSON semantics.

tasks.title: Fix visibility-aware serialization for model properties

design_review.problem: The Type_Model_Visibility spec requires that different HTTP verbs include/exclude different properties based on lifecycle visibility decorators. For example, POST should only include @visibility(Create) properties, GET responses should only include @visibility(Read) properties, PATCH should only include @visibility(Update) properties. Currently, the emitter generates a single serializer that includes ALL properties regardless of the operation context. This means: (1) PUT sends readProp (should be excluded), (2) POST sends updateProp and deleteProp (should be excluded), (3) PATCH sends createProp and deleteProp (should be excluded).

approaches.name: Generate per-operation serializers that filter by visibility
approaches.description: Generate separate serializer functions for different visibility contexts: e.g., visibilityModelCreateSerializer (only Create-visible props), visibilityModelUpdateSerializer (only Update-visible props), etc. Each operation would call the appropriate serializer based on its HTTP verb mapping. The TCGC model already provides visibility metadata per property.
pros: Correct semantics — each verb sends only relevant properties
Simple runtime behavior — just different serializer functions
cons: Multiple serializer functions per model (up to 5: create, update, read, delete, query)
Increases generated code size
Need to map HTTP verbs to visibility contexts

approaches.name: Generate a single serializer with a visibility context parameter
approaches.description: Generate one serializer function that accepts a visibility context parameter and conditionally includes/excludes properties at runtime based on the context. E.g., visibilityModelSerializer(item, context: 'create' | 'update' | 'read' | 'delete').
pros: Single serializer function — less generated code
Flexible — can handle any visibility context
cons: Runtime branching for every property on every call
More complex generated code
Harder to tree-shake unused visibility contexts
More complex to debug

approaches.name: Use TCGC's usage-based type splitting
approaches.description: TCGC already computes which properties are visible for each usage context. Use TCGC's SdkModelType.usage flags to generate separate TypeScript types (e.g., VisibilityModelInput, VisibilityModelOutput) with only the relevant properties. Each type gets its own serializer. Operations use the appropriate input/output type.
pros: Type-safe — TypeScript enforces correct property sets at compile time
TCGC does the heavy lifting of visibility computation
Matches pattern used by other language emitters (Python, Java)
cons: Generates more types — could confuse SDK users with too many model variants
Complex mapping between types
Significant refactor of model generation

design_review.chosen: Generate per-operation serializers that filter by visibility
design_review.rationale: This approach is the most practical for the current architecture. TCGC provides visibility metadata per property (property.visibility array), so the serializer generator can check which Lifecycle values a property has and include/exclude it accordingly. Per-operation serializers are simple, don't require runtime branching, and don't require splitting model types (which would be a larger refactor). The send-operation.tsx code already knows the HTTP verb, so it can select the correct serializer. This matches the approach used by flight-instructor for similar scenarios.

tasks.description:
## Summary
Fix visibility-aware serialization so that different HTTP operations only serialize properties matching their lifecycle context.

## Root Cause
The JSON serializer (`src/components/serialization/json-serializer.tsx`) generates a single serializer per model that includes ALL properties. It does not filter by visibility context (Read, Create, Update, Delete, Query).

## Fix
1. In json-serializer.tsx, when generating serializer for a model with visibility-decorated properties, generate separate serializer functions per visibility context
2. In send-operation.tsx, select the appropriate serializer based on the HTTP method:
   - GET → no body (or read-context deserializer for response)
   - POST → create-context serializer
   - PUT → create+update-context serializer
   - PATCH → update-context serializer
   - DELETE → delete-context serializer
3. In json-deserializer.tsx, generate a read-context deserializer that only includes Read-visible properties

## Scenarios Unblocked (7)

3. Run `pnpm test` for regression check

tasks.title: Fix discriminated union serializer generation

design_review.problem: The emitter generates pass-through serializers for discriminated unions (just returns the input unchanged). The Spector spec defines 4 discriminated union patterns: envelope with default kind/value, envelope with custom petType/petData, no-envelope with default discriminator, no-envelope with custom discriminator. The generated serializer needs to handle discriminator-based variant selection and envelope wrapping/unwrapping.

approaches.name: Generate switch-based serializers on the discriminator property
approaches.description: In json-union-serializer.tsx, detect discriminated unions (unions where variants share a common discriminator property with constant values) and generate a switch statement that serializes the correct variant. For envelope types, wrap the variant data in the envelope structure. For no-envelope types, serialize inline with the discriminator.
pros: Explicit and debuggable — clear switch/case for each variant
Handles both envelope and no-envelope patterns
cons: Code size grows with number of variants
Must handle both serialization and deserialization directions

approaches.name: Use the existing union-discrimination.ts infrastructure
approaches.description: The emitter already has `union-discrimination.ts` which analyzes unions to find discriminator properties and generates discrimination logic for model hierarchies. Extend this to also handle TypeSpec union types (not just model inheritance) by reusing the same discriminator detection and adding union-specific serialization templates.
cons: union-discrimination.ts is designed for model inheritance, not TypeSpec unions — may need significant adaptation
Risk of conflating two different concepts

design_review.chosen: Generate switch-based serializers on the discriminator property
design_review.rationale: Discriminated TypeSpec unions are fundamentally different from discriminated model inheritance. The union-discrimination.ts infrastructure handles polymorphic base/derived models (where all variants extend a common base type), while these discriminated unions are standalone variants distinguished by a property. A clean switch-based approach in json-union-serializer.tsx is clearer and avoids conflating concepts. The discriminator property and variant mapping can be extracted from the TCGC SdkUnionType's variants.

tasks.description:
## Summary
Fix discriminated union serializer/deserializer generation to handle envelope and no-envelope discriminated union patterns.

## Root Cause
In `src/components/serialization/json-union-serializer.tsx`, discriminated unions generate pass-through serializers (`return item`) instead of proper discriminator-aware serialization.

## Fix
1. Detect discriminated unions in json-union-serializer.tsx (unions with a shared constant-valued discriminator property)
2. Generate switch-case serializers that:
   - Read the discriminator property value
   - Call the correct variant serializer
   - For envelope types: wrap in `{kind: "...", value: serialized}` (or custom property names)
   - For no-envelope types: include discriminator inline with serialized properties
3. Generate corresponding deserializers that:
   - Read discriminator from response
   - Unwrap envelope if needed
   - Call correct variant deserializer

## Scenarios Unblocked (8)
- Type_Union_Discriminated_Envelope_Object_Default_get/put
- Type_Union_Discriminated_Envelope_Object_CustomProperties_get/put
- Type_Union_Discriminated_NoEnvelope_Default_get/put
- Type_Union_Discriminated_NoEnvelope_CustomDiscriminator_get/put

## Validation

2. Verify round-trip serialization for all 4 patterns
3. Run `pnpm test` for regression check

tasks.title: Implement JSON merge patch serialization with null preservation

design_review.problem: JSON merge patch (RFC 7386) requires that null values are preserved in the serialized body to signal property deletion. The current JSON serializer strips null/undefined values, which is correct for standard JSON but breaks merge patch semantics. The emitter has no content-type awareness in its serialization path — serializers don't know if they're being used for application/json or application/merge-patch+json.

approaches.name: Generate a separate merge-patch serializer variant
approaches.description: When TCGC marks an operation's body content-type as application/merge-patch+json, generate a dedicated serializer function (e.g., resourceMergePatchSerializer) that preserves null values instead of stripping them. The operation would call this specific serializer instead of the standard one. The serializer would use explicit null checks (value === null ? null : serialize(value)) instead of the usual undefined-stripping pattern.
cons: Duplicate serializer code for merge-patch models
Need to detect merge-patch context from TCGC types

approaches.name: Add a preserveNulls parameter to existing serializers
approaches.description: Add a boolean parameter to the generated serializer function that controls null-stripping behavior. The operation code would pass preserveNulls=true when the content-type is merge-patch+json. The serializer would conditionally include or strip nulls based on this flag.
cons: Runtime branching in every serializer call
Doesn't handle nested models correctly (flag must propagate)
More complex generated code
Harder to tree-shake

approaches.name: Make all properties optional with explicit null type in merge-patch models
approaches.description: For merge-patch models, generate TypeScript types where all updateable properties are T | null | undefined. The serializer would then naturally include null values (since they're explicitly typed) and strip undefined values (which represent 'not set').
pros: Type-safe — TypeScript enforces the null/undefined distinction
Clear semantics: null = delete, undefined = skip
cons: Requires different model types for merge-patch vs standard
More complex type generation
Users must understand null vs undefined distinction

design_review.chosen: Generate a separate merge-patch serializer variant
design_review.rationale: A dedicated merge-patch serializer is the cleanest approach. TCGC's SdkHttpOperation already provides the content type, so the emitter can detect merge-patch context at code generation time. The dedicated serializer preserves null values explicitly, which is simple to implement and doesn't require runtime branching or model type changes. This matches how the legacy emitter handles merge-patch and avoids polluting the standard serializer code path.

tasks.description:

tasks.title: Implement JSONL streaming support

design_review.problem: The emitter has no JSONL streaming support. The spec requires generating code that can send and receive newline-delimited JSON (application/jsonl) as streaming request/response bodies. Currently excluded via .testignore. This is a medium-sized feature requiring changes to content-type handling, response body generation, and possibly new runtime helpers.

approaches.name: AsyncIterable-based streaming with per-line JSON parsing
approaches.description: Generate operations that return AsyncIterable<T> for streaming responses and accept AsyncIterable<T> for streaming requests. For responses, read the body as a stream, split on newlines, and parse each line as JSON. For requests, iterate the input and write each object as a JSON line. Add a static helper (jsonl-helpers.tsx) with parseJsonlStream() and writeJsonlStream() utilities.
pros: Works in both Node.js and browser
cons: Requires changes to operation return type generation
Need to handle backpressure and partial lines
More complex than simple request/response

approaches.name: ReadableStream-based streaming with transform
approaches.description: Generate operations that return ReadableStream<T> using the Web Streams API. Use a TransformStream to split and parse JSONL data. This is more browser-native but less ergonomic than AsyncIterable.
cons: Less ergonomic — users must use .getReader()
Not all Node.js versions support Web Streams natively
More complex API surface

design_review.chosen: AsyncIterable-based streaming with per-line JSON parsing
design_review.rationale: AsyncIterable is the standard pattern for streaming in JavaScript/TypeScript and is supported natively in all modern runtimes. It's more ergonomic (for-await-of) and matches patterns used by Azure SDK and other TypeScript client libraries. The implementation can use a ReadableStream internally but expose AsyncIterable<T> to consumers.

tasks.description:
## Summary
Implement JSONL (application/jsonl) streaming support for request and response bodies.

## Scope
This is a medium-sized feature (~400-500 lines of new code):

1. **Content-type detection** — Detect application/jsonl in TCGC types
2. **Operation return type** — Generate AsyncIterable<T> for streaming responses instead of T
3. **Request body** — Generate streaming request body from AsyncIterable<T>
4. **Static helper** — Create `src/components/static-helpers/jsonl-helpers.tsx` with parseJsonlStream() and writeJsonlStream()
5. **Remove from .testignore** — Remove 'streaming' from test/e2e/.testignore
6. **E2E tests** — Create `test/e2e/http/streaming/jsonl/main.test.ts`

## Scenarios Unblocked (2)
- Streaming_Jsonl_Basic_receive
- Streaming_Jsonl_Basic_send

## Notes
- Neither the legacy emitter nor the reference emitters have streaming support
- The ts-http-runtime package may need streaming utilities added
tasks.blocked_reason: Requires TCGC version with SdkStreamMetadata support. Installed TCGC v0.65.3 does not have streamMetadata property on SdkBodyParameter or SdkMethodResponse. The submodule TCGC (v0.65.2-dev) has this feature but it has not been released. Unblock by upgrading @azure-tools/typespec-client-generator-core to a version that exports SdkStreamMetadata.

These test JSDoc/documentation formatting in generated code, not runtime behavior. Neither the legacy nor any reference emitter tests these.

approaches.name: Mark as not-applicable in coverage
approaches.description: Formally annotate these scenarios as 'not-applicable' in the spector coverage configuration so they don't count against the coverage percentage. Documentation formatting is a code-generation cosmetic concern, not a runtime behavior test.
pros: Honestly reflects that these aren't client functionality tests
Improves coverage percentage without false work
cons: Documentation quality isn't verified by e2e tests
If a customer cares about JSDoc formatting, no test catches regressions

approaches.name: Implement documentation formatting and test via scenario tests
approaches.description: Support markdown formatting (bold, italic, lists) in generated JSDoc comments. Test via scenario tests (not e2e) that verify the generated documentation output matches expected formatting. Remove 'documentation' from .testignore.
cons: Significant work for cosmetic feature
Scenario tests are better suited than e2e for this
Low ROI

design_review.rationale: Documentation formatting is a cosmetic concern that's better tested via scenario tests (which verify generated output) than e2e tests (which verify runtime behavior). The 6 scenarios should be marked not-applicable in the spector coverage configuration. If documentation formatting support is desired, it should be tracked as a separate feature with scenario test validation, not e2e.
